title: Visual Working Memory Capacity Models: Slot vs Resource Debate
description: Concept map showing the competing theoretical frameworks for explaining VWM capacity limits and their empirical evidence

nodes.label: VWM Capacity Limit
nodes.definition: The maximum number of objects (~3-4) that visual working memory can simultaneously represent

nodes.label: Slot Model
nodes.definition: Discrete storage mechanism with fixed number (~3-4) of independent slots, each holding one complete object with all features

nodes.label: Resource Model
nodes.definition: Flexible allocation of shared representational resources distributed across all items in display; capacity limit from finite resource pool

nodes.label: Change Detection Paradigm
nodes.definition: Experimental method: view array → delay → test array; measure errors and RT to estimate K (capacity)

nodes.label: Set Size Effect
nodes.definition: Performance plateau when display contains >3-4 items; adding more items doesn't reduce K

nodes.label: Precision-Load Tradeoff
nodes.definition: Memory precision for individual items decreases as more items must be stored; evidence for resource allocation

nodes.label: All-or-Nothing Storage
nodes.definition: Items are either fully remembered with all features or completely forgotten; supports slot model predictions

nodes.label: Flexible Resolution Storage
nodes.definition: Items stored with variable precision depending on attentional resources allocated; all items partially stored

nodes.label: Feature Binding Problem
nodes.definition: How visual features (color, orientation, position) are bound together into unified objects in VWM

nodes.label: Attentional Control
nodes.definition: Role of attention in determining which items enter VWM and how resources are allocated across items

nodes.label: Cowan's Integrated Model
nodes.definition: Synthesis of slot and resource mechanisms; capacity limit reflects interaction of discrete focusing and flexible allocation

nodes.label: Iconic Memory Buffer
nodes.definition: Early visual sensory store with high capacity but short duration (~500ms); feeds into VWM

nodes.label: Visual Cortex Architecture
nodes.definition: Neural substrate for VWM; retinotopic maps and feature-selective neurons constrain capacity

edges.label: explains via discrete storage
edges.evidence: Luck & Vogel showed constant K~3.5 across set sizes; compatible with fixed-slot interpretation

edges.label: explains via resource allocation
edges.evidence: Zhang & Luck showed precision increases with fewer items; compatible with shared-resource interpretation

edges.label: mechanistically incompatible
edges.evidence: Slot model predicts all-or-nothing; resource model predicts variable precision. Both fit data but via different processes

edges.label: quantifies capacity via K estimate
edges.evidence: Luck & Vogel's paradigm became standard method for measuring VWM capacity

edges.label: plateau in performance consistent with fixed slots
edges.evidence: K plateaus at 3-4 even when set size increases to 8+; suggests limited discrete slots

edges.label: precision decreases with load; supports resource sharing
edges.evidence: Zhang & Luck (2008) showed inverse relationship between items and per-item precision

edges.label: all-or-nothing pattern matches discrete slots
edges.evidence: Memory for items either complete or absent; limited intermediate precision states

edges.label: variable precision matches resource allocation
edges.evidence: Items stored with continuous range of precisions depending on task demands

edges.label: each slot binds multiple features into unified object
edges.evidence: Luck & Vogel assumed feature binding occurs automatically within slots

edges.label: attentional control gates capacity bottleneck
edges.evidence: Cowan (2001) showed capacity limit reflects attention-based selection mechanism

edges.label: attention allocates resources across items
edges.evidence: Zhang & Luck linked attentional focus to precision allocation across items

edges.label: incorporates discrete focusing mechanism
edges.evidence: Cowan's model includes 4±1 discrete slots as focus component

edges.label: incorporates flexible resource allocation
edges.evidence: Cowan's model includes continuous resource distribution around focused items

edges.label: high-capacity sensory buffer compressed into VWM
edges.evidence: Luck & Vogel discussed how iconic memory (~10 items) is bottlenecked into VWM (~3-4)

edges.label: neural architecture limits capacity
edges.evidence: Cowan argued capacity reflects cortical organization; not cognitive choice

edges.label: paradigm reveals plateau in K
edges.evidence: Classic finding from repeated change detection experiments

edges.label: RT/accuracy can reveal precision changes
edges.evidence: Zhang & Luck extended change detection to measure precision curves

source_papers.luck_vogel1997: Luck, S. J., & Vogel, E. K. (1997). The capacity of visual working memory for features and conjunctions. Nature, 390(6657), 279-281.
source_papers.cowan2001: Cowan, N. (2001). The magical number 4 in short-term memory. Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 24(1), 87-185.
source_papers.zhang_luck2008: Zhang, W., & Luck, S. J. (2008). Discrete fixed-resolution representations in visual working memory. Nature, 453(7192), 233-235.

metadata.purpose: Visualize competing theoretical explanations for VWM capacity limits and their supporting evidence
